1:
אֱלֹהֵי צִדְקִי (ʾĕlōhê ṣidqî, "God of my righteousness") — not "my righteous God" (which would be ʾĕlōhay haṣṣaddîq) but "the God who is the source/vindicator of my righteousness." Ṣedeq/ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) here has a forensic sense: vindication, the declaration that one's cause is just. God is appealed to as the one who will adjudicate David's case.

בַּצָּר הִרְחַבְתָּ לִּי (baṣṣār hirḥabtā llî, "you freed me when I was in distress") — literally "in the tight place you enlarged me." Ṣār (distress) and rāḥab (wide/broad) are antonyms: God takes the person from constriction to wide-open space. The spatial metaphor of salvation as expansion is common in the Psalms.
2: בְּנֵי אִישׁ (bĕnê ʾîsh, "O men") — literally "sons of man" using ʾîsh (distinguished man, man of rank), not ʾādām (common humanity). The address is to the distinguished and influential, those who should know better. כָּזָב (kāzāb, "lies") — falsehood, deception; the accusers are those who "love what is worthless (rîq) and pursue lies." Rîq appears again from Psalms 2:1 — the futile, empty plotting.
3: הִפְלָה יְהוָה חָסִיד לוֹ (hiphlāh YHWH ḥāsîd lô, "the LORD has set apart the faithful for himself") — hiphlāh (hiphil of pālāh): to make distinct, to set apart as remarkable or special. The ḥāsîd (faithful/godly one) is the one characterized by ḥesed (covenant loyalty) — not merely religious but relationally faithful to the covenant. The LORD has specially distinguished this person as his own.
4:
רִגְזוּ וְאַל-תֶּחֱטָאוּ (rigzû wĕʾal-teḥĕṭāʾû, "Tremble with awe and do not sin") — Paul cites this in Ephesians 4:26 ("be angry but do not sin"), and the LXX translates it ὀργίζεσθε καὶ μὴ ἁμαρτάνετε. The Hebrew rāgaz primarily means to tremble/shake (used of earthquakes and of the earth's response to the divine presence); its translation as "anger" may import a secondary meaning. The MKT "tremble with awe" recovers the primary sense: physical trembling in response to God's reality.

אִמְרוּ בִלְבַבְכֶם (ʾimrû bilbabkem, "search your hearts") — literally "say in your hearts." The heart (lēbāb) is the organ of thought, will, and intention in Hebrew anthropology — not primarily emotion but cognition and decision. On your beds (מִשְׁכְּבוֹתֵיכֶם), in silence (וְדֹמּוּ, from dāmam: to be still/silent).
5: זִבְחוּ זִבְחֵי-צֶדֶק (zibḥû zibḥê-ṣedeq, "Offer right sacrifices") — literally "sacrifices of righteousness," using ṣedeq as a genitive of quality: sacrifices characterized by rightness. Not merely technically correct ritual but offering from a right internal orientation. The instruction parallels Psalms 51:17-19 where David learns that God does not desire sacrifice per se but a broken and contrite heart.
6: נְסָה-עָלֵינוּ אוֹר פָּנֶיךָ (nĕsāh-ʿālênû ʾôr pānêkā, "Let the light of your face shine on us") — the verb nāsāh (to lift up, to display) and the phrase ʾôr pānêkā (light of your face) both come from the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:25: "The LORD make his face shine on you"). The communal prayer invokes the priestly blessing pattern: the turned and shining face of God is the answer to the question "who will show us any good?"
7: נָתַתָּה שִׂמְחָה בְלִבִּי (nātattāh śimḥāh bĕlibbî, "You have placed more joy in my heart") — the joy is given (nātan, perfect: completed gift). It is not self-generated. The comparison is explicit: more than "when their grain and wine overflow" (מֵעֵת דְּגָנָם וְתִירוֹשָׁם רָבּוּ). The joy of the harvest (the maximum moment of agrarian celebration) is less than the joy God places internally. The external versus internal contrast is sharp.
8: בְּשָׁלוֹם יַחְדָּו אֶשְׁכְּבָה וְאִישָׁן (bĕshālôm yaḥdāw ʾeshkĕbāh wĕʾîshān, "In peace I will lie down and sleep") — shālôm (completeness/peace) as the condition of sleep: rest that is whole, not anxious. Yaḥdāw (together/at once) intensifies: simultaneously, without delay. לְבַדָד (lĕbaddō, "you alone") — not divine plurality but exclusive reliance: "you, you alone, O LORD, cause me to dwell in safety." The security is not social, political, or personal — only divine.
